author: @b1rdp3rson67
text: Does any1 know if graphites electrical generation scales up with heat? Let’s hypothetically say it produces 50w at 100celsius, will it continue to scale upwards to say 500w at 1000c, or will heat resistance start pulling back conductivity? If so there are serious implications of being able to use fire in a backcountry situation to produce significant power generation, I know biolite and some others utilize this, but they ain’t great. Tried to make this years back but my insulators kept melting and I got lazy. Imagine having a continually upscaling thermoelectric generator positioned outside of the suns corona or in volcanic vents, literally free infinite energy.

author: @wanderingzanzey2126
text:
I worked on a project with Unilin (R&D branch of Mohawk), we used a conductive carbon ink absorbed into a melamine impregnated cellulose paper. Then used that as one of the layers in tongue and groove flooring. Heat pressing everything together to set the melamine. We used carbon inductive coils to transfer power from a thin sub-floor layer operating at 1MHz, also made of parallel connected carbon inductive coils with a ferritic bottom to force the magnetic flux to be half the thickness of the floor board. 

This project worked well, but when COVID hit the project was scrapped. Mohawk was thinking of releasing "smart" flooring. Capable of high-resolution zoned sensing, heating, and power transfer capabilities. Imagine a standing light fixture with no cables ;). Would've been great.

author: @thedevilinthecircuit
text: Makers of electric guitars have been using this conductive carbon paint for more than 25 years to shield the pickup and control cavities from EMI/RF.  The nature of gain circuitry in amplifiers demands these sorts of measures.  Some makers use adhesive copper foil with conductive adhesive (for continuity between layers), but copper tape is more labor intensive and increases production costs.

author: @jamesross1003
text: What makes me excited about this is that if you can find 2 different paints with the right impedances(dissimilar), then you may be able to make some type of TEG out of it.  This same idea has been done before using a graphene based ink and a graphene oxide and graphite paint.  Thanks for the video Rob!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @@dawver4829  I suppose that it is possible as two different graphite composites(ie...pencil lead hardness is one example) can be used as a TEG, though how much temperature difference might not be sufficient as a cooling panel.  It might be worth investigating.  With these types of paints we are only limited as to our imaginations.  Nice possible idea there you have, now go for it and tell us how it works out!
